chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Introduction to Computer Science Concepts and Programming. (3-2). Credit 4. Computation to enhance problem solving abilities; understanding how people communicate with computers, and how computing affects society; computational thinking; software design principles, including algorithm design, data representation, abstraction, modularity, structured and object oriented programming, documentation, testing, portability, and maintenance; understanding programs' abilities and limitations; development and execution programs.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

• Use a text editor to program Java code.
• Use a Java compiler to produce executable software.
• Identify the basic syntax and semantics of the Java programming language.
• Use appropriate commenting, layout, and naming to communicate code intent
• Understand the primitive data types built into the Java language.
• Understand the concepts of Java classes and objects.
• Understand the concepts of lifetime, scope, and the initialization mechanism of variables.
• Implement Java code branching using if or switch statements.
• Program loops with while, for, and do statements.
• Make use of arrays to store and process lists of data.
• Assemble data and methods into classes following the software engineering principles of encapsulation and data hiding.
• Organize Java code following the software engineering principles of modularity and abstraction.
• Create Java I/O interfaces
• Read, interpret, analyze, and explain introductory Java programs.
• Test and evaluate introductory Java programs

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: None

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Grading Scale
The grading will be on an absolute scale mentioned below:

Overall Grading Scale
| Rounded Total | Letter Grade |
|---|---|
| Total ≥ 90% | A |
| 80% ≤ Total < 90% | B |
| 70% ≤ Total < 80% | C |
| 60% ≤ Total < 70% | D |
| Total < 60% | F |

chunks.title: Component Weights
chunks.content:
The grade is calculated based on the following course components:

Course Component Weights
| Component | Weight |
|---|---|
| Class Activities | 5% |
| Discussion Posts | 5% |
| Reading Quizzes | 5% |
| Homeworks | 10% |
| Lab Assignments | 15% |
| Exam 1 | 15% |
| Exam 2 | 20% |
| Final Exam | 25% |

chunks.title: Class Activities
chunks.content:
• Class activities will be graded based on participation rather than correctness.
• They will only be available for completion during class time.
• In case of excused absences, the activity will be marked excused. Canvas processes excused absences by increasing the weight of the unexcused activities.
• Three unexcused absences are allowed during the semester. This will be processed at the end of the semester.

chunks.title: Lab Assignments
chunks.content:
• Labs are going to be group activities where attendance is required to earn full credit.
• Groups will be assigned in Canvas and will be changed every 4 weeks.
• If you are absent from lab, you can complete the assignment individually with a 20% penalty.
• In case of excused absences, lab assignment can be completed individually without penalty.

chunks.title: Homework
chunks.content:
• Homeworks are individual assignments and must be completed.
• Collaboration with other students and the use of AI tools are strictly prohibited.

chunks.title: Exam 1
chunks.content:
• At the end of the semester, Exam 1 score will be replaced by average of all three exams if the average is higher.
• This policy allows you to partially offset a low score.

chunks.title: Exam 2
chunks.content:
• At the end of the semester, Exam 2 score will be replaced by average of all three exams if the average is higher.
• This policy allows you to partially offset a low score.

chunks.title: Regrade Requests
chunks.content: If you want to challenge any grading, you must do so within one week of grade release. Regrade period for assignments published after week 13 will be shorter and will be announced at the time of grade release. There may be no regrading period for the final exam.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
• Late submissions are only allowed for Homeworks.
• There will be 10% per day penalty for late submissions.
• There may be no late submission period for the final homework of the semester.

chunks.title: Weekly Topics
chunks.content:
Weekly Topics
| Week | Topic |
|---|---|
| 1 | Course Introduction, Variables and Printing |
| 2 | Numeric data types, Math library, Characters, Defining simple methods |
| 3 | Strings, Formatted Output, Boolean Type and Basic Conditionals |
| 4 | Multiple conditions, condition order, nested conditions, loop basics |
| 5 | For Loops, Exam 1. |
| 6 | Arrays and for loops. Loops patterns with arrays. |
| 7 | Multi-dimensional arrays and Debugging. |
| 8 | Introduction to Objects. Objects with methods. |
| 9 | Spring Break |
| 10 | Exam 2. Object Oriented Design, Relations between objects. |
| 11 | Dynamic dispatch and access control in object-oriented programming. |
| 12 | Practice with object oriented design. Exceptions. Exceptions control flow and defining exc |
| 13 | File handling and user interfaces for a program. |
| 14 | Graphical User Interfaces (GUI) |
| 15 | Graphical User Interfaces (GUI) continued |
| 16 | Final Exam |

chunks.title: Homework Deadlines
chunks.content: Homeworks will be released on Monday. Typical deadline for the Homeworks will be Sunday night. Any change will be announced through canvas.

chunks.title: Lab Assignment Deadlines
chunks.content: Lab assignments will be released at the start of your weekly lab sessions. The deadline for the lab assignment will be midnight the day after the Lab. The assignments are designed to be completed within the lab session, but the deadline allows you to seek help if you run into any problems. Any changes will be announced through canvas.

chunks.title: Exam Dates
chunks.content:
• Exam 1: Thursday, Feb 12, 2026. In class.
• Exam 2: Thursday, Mar 19, 2026, In class.
• Final Exam: May 1st, 2026. 1:00 p.m. – 3:00 p.m.

Final exam times are based on the TAMU official final exam schedule. If there is an error here, we will adhere to the TAMU official schedule.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the students themselves. Students should not have another person/entity do the writing of any substantive portion of an assignment for them, which includes hiring a person or a company to write assignments and using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT.

chunks.content:
Technology Services (IT) - Main Campus
Hours: 24/7

Call/Chat/Email/visit: https://it.tamu.edu/help

chunks.title: Learning Resources
chunks.content:
Canvas
Dashboard for the entire course. This will link out to appropriate resources.

Campuswire
• Campuswire will be the main forum to get help with homework and lab activities.
• You will be added to the discussion forum at the start of the semester.
• Whenever possible, post your questions publicly so that all students can benefit from it. Please do not post your code and solutions in public posts. Public posts can also be answered by other students so you should have a shorter response time for them.
• We will endorse good student responses.
• You have the option to post anonymously. Your post will only be anonymous to students. Instructors will be able to see your name and id on the posts.
• Please be respectful.
• Private messages can be posed to all instructors or an individual instructor.
• Private messages to individual instructors are more likely to be overlooked and should not be used. Send an email directly to the instructor.
• Posts directed to instructors that would benefit the entire class and should be visible to all students will be updated so that the post is visible by the entire class.

chunks.title: Google Drive
chunks.content: Google folder will be used to share lecture notes and in-class examples. You must be logged into your TAMU Google account to access these materials or submit Google Forms. Your TAMU Google account will have to be the default account in your browser, or you can open an incognito window and log in from there. It might take up to 24 hours after registering for the class to be able to access materials in the Google shared folder.

chunks.title: Zoom
chunks.content: Zoom will be used for online office hours, and meetings with instructors and teaching assistants. Links will be provided as needed.
